ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no specific entry for a bundled fried chicken and spaghetti meal. FOODON_00002490 (fried chicken food product) is used as the closest defensible anchor for the primary protein component. The spaghetti component is not separately coded.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP (Chicken, fried) is used as the primary anchor for the dominant protein component. No composite bundle code exists in FoodEx2 for fried chicken plus sweet spaghetti; the pasta component is not separately anchored.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this composite fast-food bundle meal. SNOMED CT does not maintain granular fast-food composite dish codes.

serving.description: 1 set meal (2 pieces Chickenjoy, 1 regular spaghetti, 1 drink)
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard Jollibee bundle set comprising two pieces of bone-in crispy fried chicken, one regular serving of sweet-style spaghetti with meat sauce, and one sugar-sweetened soft drink, totalling approximately 850 g.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Fast Foods, fried chicken, boneless pieces, plain; and Fast Foods, spaghetti with meat sauce
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA entry exists for the Jollibee Chickenjoy and Spaghetti Bundle Meal as a composite. Per-100g values were derived by weight-averaging USDA FoodData Central entries for fast-food fried chicken (bone-in, breaded) and fast-food spaghetti with meat sauce, with the soft drink component contributing primarily water, sugar, and energy. The input record's per-100g values (energy 176 kcal, protein 9.4 g, fat 10.0 g, carbohydrate 11.8 g) are consistent with this weighted composite and are retained with medium confidence. Monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fat data are not available for this composite from the approved source and are set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; University of Sydney GI Database — spaghetti (white, boiled)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for the Jollibee Chickenjoy and Spaghetti Bundle Meal. GI is estimated as a weighted composite anchored to the dominant carbohydrate source: white spaghetti (GI 46, University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care 31(12):2281-2283). The fried chicken component contributes negligible carbohydrate and does not materially shift the composite GI. The sweet tomato sauce and soft drink contribute added sugar but the overall carbohydrate-weighted GI of the meal remains in the low range (~46) because the pasta dominates the carbohydrate fraction and pasta has a well-documented low GI due to its dense starch matrix. A composite GI of 46 (low) is therefore applied. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI 46 × carbohydrate per 850 g serving ~100 g) / 100 = 46.0 g GL.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium from breaded fried chicken, processed meat sauce, and hot dog slices; compounded by sugar-sweetened beverage
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid this meal due to its high sodium content (~2,500 mg per 850 g serving) from fried chicken breading, processed meat sauce, and hot dog components; the sugar-sweetened soft drink further elevates cardiovascular risk and should be replaced with plain water.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycaemic load from refined spaghetti, sweet tomato sauce, and sugar-sweetened soft drink; significant added sugar intake
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with type 2 diabetes should avoid this bundle meal because the combination of refined white spaghetti, sweet tomato-based sauce, and a sugar-sweetened soft drink produces a high glycaemic load (~46 g GL per serving), causing postprandial blood glucose elevation; substituting the soft drink with water and requesting wholemeal pasta would substantially reduce glycaemic impact.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from deep-fried chicken skin and breading, trans fat (0.12 g/100g), high sodium, and processed meat components
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit or avoid this meal due to its saturated fat (~25 g per serving), trans fat (~1 g per serving), high sodium (~2,500 mg per serving), and ultra-processed meat components, all of which are independently associated with increased LDL cholesterol, arterial inflammation, and elevated cardiovascular risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) from deep-frying, trans fat, and pro-inflammatory processed meat components
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this meal as deep-fried chicken generates high levels of advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that promote systemic inflammation, and the trans fat (0.12 g/100g) and processed hot dog components further exacerbate pro-inflammatory pathways; grilled chicken without breading is a preferable alternative.

llm_training.natural_language_description: The Chickenjoy and Spaghetti Bundle Meal is a popular fast-food combo offered by Jollibee, a Filipino quick-service restaurant chain with outlets across Southeast Asia including Singapore. The bundle typically comprises two pieces of Chickenjoy — Jollibee's signature bone-in, skin-on fried chicken coated in a seasoned crispy breading and deep-fried until golden — alongside one regular serving of Jolly Spaghetti, a distinctively Filipino-style sweet spaghetti featuring a tomato-based meat sauce sweetened with banana ketchup and studded with sliced hot dogs and ground meat, served over boiled white spaghetti noodles. The meal is completed with a sugar-sweetened soft drink. The chicken is characterised by its juicy interior, crispy exterior, and mildly savoury-sweet seasoning. The spaghetti sauce is notably sweeter than Italian-style preparations, reflecting Filipino taste preferences. Nutritionally, the bundle is energy-dense at approximately 176 kcal per 100 g, with a full 850 g serving delivering roughly 1,500 kcal, 80 g fat, 100 g carbohydrate, and 2,500 mg sodium. The meal is ultra-processed, high in saturated fat, trans fat, sodium, and added sugar, making it unsuitable for individuals managing hypertension, type 2 diabetes, cardiovascular disease, or rheumatoid arthritis. It is classified as halal. The glycaemic load per serving is approximately 46 g GL, driven by refined pasta and sweet sauce; the pasta's dense starch matrix keeps the composite GI in the low range despite the sweet components. This record is relevant for dietary counselling, fast-food nutritional analysis, and Southeast Asian food culture documentation.

Chickenjoy spaghetti set meal calories Singapore

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for the Jollibee Chickenjoy and Spaghetti Bundle Meal as a composite dish. Per-100g values are estimated by weight-averaging USDA FoodData Central entries for fast-food fried chicken and fast-food spaghetti with meat sauce, consistent with the input record's values. GI is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database data for white spaghetti (GI 46) as the dominant carbohydrate source. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite fast-food bundle meal is not available in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB database as of 2024. potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g set to -1: micronutrient and moisture data for this composite bundle meal are not reported in USDA FoodData Central for the specific Jollibee formulation. Input record listed country_of_origin as Singapore and primary_cuisine as Burgers — both corrected: Jollibee is a Filipino brand and the cuisine is Filipino fast food; multilingual names are provided for Singaporean context per G6 rules as the food is served in Singapore. Input record's health_suitability conditions used non-standard keys (diabetes, hypertension, rheumatoid_arthritis) — remapped to type2_diabetes, hypertension, and rheumatoid_arthritis with cardiovascular_disease added as required by Q7. Trans fat is 0.12 g/100g (~1.0 g per 850 g serving) — clinically significant pro-inflammatory amount. Portion size of 850 g is sourced from GrabFood listing and retained as the default serving size. Iteration 3 fix: gi_category corrected from 'medium' to 'low' to match glycemic_index 46; GI value revised from 55 to 46 to align with the University of Sydney GI Database anchor for white spaghetti (GI 46), which is the dominant carbohydrate source in this bundle; glycemic_load_per_serving updated from 55.0 to 46.0 accordingly.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Corrected country_of_origin from Singapore to Philippines (served in Singapore); corrected primary_cuisine from Burgers to Filipino Fast Food.
Iteration 1: Added mandatory multilingual names (Chinese simplified, Chinese traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) for Singaporean serving context per G6.
Iteration 1: Added cardiovascular_disease health context entry required by Q7; remapped diabetes key to type2_diabetes with correct human_condition_name.
Iteration 1: Added GI estimate anchored to University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2008) with full analogous-estimate documentation.
Iteration 1: Set monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g to -1 with documented justification in notes.
Iteration 1: Documented all -1 fields in notes per G3 requirement.
Iteration 1: Added defensible FoodOn and FoodEx2 ontology anchors with gap notes; set SNOMED CT to null with gap note.
Iteration 1: Verified aliases_by_language consistency with flat aliases array per q8 self-check rule.
Iteration 2: Corrected ontology.foodon_match_type and ontology.foodex2_match_type from 'approximate' to 'closest_parent' as per G5 hard gate feedback.

Iteration 3: Corrected gi_category from 'medium' to 'low' to match glycemic_index; revised glycemic_index from 55 to 46 to align with University of Sydney GI Database anchor for white spaghetti (GI 46) as the dominant carbohydrate source; updated glycemic_load_per_serving from 55.0 to 46.0; updated gi_source notes and type2_diabetes guidance to reflect corrected GL value.
